title: Workshop 6: AI for Curriculum Development
text:
Overview (8 min)
Start from the objective, not the slide. Good course design works backward: decide what students should be able to do, then design the assessment that measures it, then build the activities and materials that get them there. AI is fast at drafting at every step—but you bring the disciplinary judgment about what is correct, current, and worth teaching.
Three tools, and what each is best at.

ChatGPT Edu (Drexel-approved) — open-ended drafting and refining: learning objectives, session outlines, case studies, discussion prompts, and rubrics. Build a custom GPT (from Workshop 3)—a “Lesson Planner” or “Rubric Builder”—once, and reuse and share it with colleagues. Use GPT-5.5 when it appears in your model picker.
Microsoft 365 Copilot — works inside the files you already use: turn an outline into a Word syllabus, a PowerPoint deck, or an Excel grading rubric. The new Teach experience builds structured, standards-aligned unit and lesson plans from a topic. (Teach is rolling out and is oriented toward K–12 education licenses; check what is available in your Drexel Microsoft 365 account. The build-from-a-topic structure still applies to course units.)
NotebookLM (Google) — source-grounded: upload your own readings, lecture notes, or a chapter and generate study guides, quizzes, briefing docs, and Audio Overviews, all with inline citations to your sources. Excellent for student-facing materials—but a consumer/Google tool, so treat it as Low Risk Data only unless Drexel has approved it, and use public or non-sensitive sources. Confirm on Drexel’s AI Tools page.







TipThe Mental Model: Backward Design



Objectives → assessment → activities → materials. Ask AI to help at each step, in that order. If you start by generating slides, you get content with no target. If you start from the objective, everything you build has something to align to—and something to check it against.

title: Workshop 6: AI for Curriculum Development
section: Ethics & Governance: Integrity, Accuracy, Accessibility, and Student Data (15 min)
text:
Ethics & Governance: Integrity, Accuracy, Accessibility, and Student Data (15 min)
The thread continues, now in the classroom. Earlier workshops covered how AI fails (W1), verification and disclosure (W2), and data and consent in team use (W3). Teaching adds its own duties: you are handing materials to students who will trust them, and you are handling student data.
Model the integrity you ask of students. Set a clear AI policy for your course, and be transparent with students about where and how you used AI to build it. The standard you hold students to is more credible when your own use is open and intentional.
Verify everything you hand to students. AI invents facts, citations, statistics, and can misstate disciplinary content with confidence. Every figure, citation, and claim in a teaching material needs to be checked against the source—students will not catch errors you miss. The rule from Workshop 2 applies: if you can’t open it, you can’t cite it.
Design for accessibility and equity. AI can help here—alt text for images, plain-language versions, adjusting reading level, drafting captions. It can also bake in bias or produce materials that don’t meet accessibility standards. Check that examples and images are inclusive and that what you publish is accessible to every student.
Protect student data (FERPA). Protected student records—grades, rosters, identifiable student work—must not go into a tool that isn’t approved for that data. Grading or giving feedback on identifiable student work requires an approved tool and an approved use case; otherwise de-identify first. For NotebookLM and other consumer tools, use public or synthetic content only.






ImportantBefore You Publish a Teaching Material



Four checks before it reaches students: Accurate? (every fact and citation verified against the source). Accessible? (readable, captioned, alt-text where needed). Inclusive? (examples and images represent your students). Compliant? (no protected student data went into an unapproved tool). If any answer is no, fix it before you hand it out.

title: Workshop 6: AI for Curriculum Development
section: Activity 1: From Objectives to an Aligned Session
text:
Activity 1: From Objectives to an Aligned Session

Activity 1: From Objectives to an Aligned Session

Duration: ~19 minutes | Format: Hands-on, choose your track. Use non-sensitive content appropriate under Drexel’s Terms of Use.

Goal: Go from a learning objective to an aligned session plan, an activity, and a draft assessment—backward design, with AI doing the drafting.

Track A — ChatGPT Edu (drafting and refining)

Write one or two learning objectives for a session you teach (non-sensitive).
Use a PTCF prompt (from Workshop 1):

Persona: You are an instructional designer for graduate public health courses. Task: Draft a 50-minute session plan aligned to these objectives, with one active-learning activity and a short formative assessment (a check for understanding). Context: [your objectives; the audience; any constraints]. Format: Objectives, timed outline, activity instructions, and the assessment, in plain language.

Iterate: ask for a rubric for the assessment, a short case study, or three discussion questions. Push it: “Make this more active,” “Does the assessment actually measure objective 2?”



Track B — Microsoft 365 Copilot (build it in your files)

Drop your outline into Word and ask Copilot to format it as a session plan or syllabus section.
In PowerPoint, ask Copilot to draft a slide deck from the outline; in Excel, ask it to build a grading rubric as a table.
If the Teach experience is available in your account, generate a structured unit or lesson plan from your topic, then refine it.



Reflection

Where did the AI’s draft need your disciplinary judgment to be correct or current?
Did the assessment actually measure the objective—or just stay on the topic?

title: Workshop 6: AI for Curriculum Development
section: Activity 2: Turn Your Readings into Student Materials
text:
Activity 2: Turn Your Readings into Student Materials

Activity 2: Turn Your Readings into Student Materials

Duration: ~13 minutes | Format: Hands-on, individual. Use public or non-sensitive sources only.

Goal: Turn your own sources into student-facing study materials that are grounded in—and cite back to—those sources.






WarningData Note — Read First



NotebookLM is a Google tool. Treat it as Low Risk Data only unless Drexel has approved it for higher classifications. For this activity, use public or fully non-sensitive sources—an open-access article, your own published slides, a public report. No student data, no unpublished or sensitive material. Confirm the current status on Drexel’s AI Tools page.



Instructions

In NotebookLM, create a notebook and upload one to three public sources (an open-access paper, your own slides, a public dataset summary).
Generate a study guide and a set of quiz questions from the sources. Ask for a glossary of key terms.
Generate an Audio Overview—a short, conversational summary students could listen to before class.
Check every output against the sources. NotebookLM cites where each point came from; you confirm the accuracy, emphasis, and tone before anything reaches a student.



Discussion

Which student material saved you the most time to produce?
Where did grounding in your own sources beat a general chatbot’s answer?

title: Workshop 6: AI for Curriculum Development
text:
Wrap-Up (3 min)

Start from objectives; let AI help build aligned activities, assessments, and materials—you keep the disciplinary judgment.
ChatGPT Edu for open drafting and reusable custom GPTs; Microsoft 365 Copilot for building inside Office files; NotebookLM for source-grounded student materials (public data only).
Teaching ethics: integrity and disclosure, verified accuracy, accessibility, and FERPA.

Try this before the term: pick one session you teach, write its objectives, and use ChatGPT Edu to draft an aligned activity and a quick formative assessment. Verify it, then teach it.






WarningData Classification and Student Records



Confirm the approved data level for each tool on Drexel’s AI Tools page before putting real material into it. Never enter protected student records (grades, rosters, identifiable student work) into a tool that isn’t approved for that data. NotebookLM and other consumer tools: public or synthetic content only.

title: Workshop 4: AI for Documents & Visuals (Adobe)
text:
Overview (8 min)
What you have. Drexel provides Adobe tools with AI features. Two AI capabilities matter most for this audience:

Acrobat AI Assistant — works inside a PDF to summarize it, answer questions about it, and generate text (a summary, an email, key points) with links back to the source passages.
Firefly / Adobe Express — generative image tools (text-to-image, generative fill and expand) and templates for flyers, infographics, social posts, and slide visuals. Drexel’s tool page notes monthly generative-credit limits.

Data rule for these tools. Drexel lists Acrobat AI Assistant, PDF Spaces, Creative Cloud, and Express as usable with Moderate Risk Data. Confirm the current classification on Drexel’s AI Tools page before putting real documents into Acrobat AI Assistant.
“Commercially safe” generation. Adobe trains Firefly on licensed and public-domain content and positions its output as commercially safe to use. That matters when a graphic ends up in a published report, grant, or public campaign. Still review any generated image for accuracy and appropriateness—generative tools can produce misleading or off-tone visuals.






TipThe Split



Reach for Acrobat AI Assistant when the work is trapped in a PDF. Reach for Firefly / Express when you need a visual and don’t have a designer. Both are about getting a usable draft fast, then applying your judgment.

title: Workshop 4: AI for Documents & Visuals (Adobe)
section: Ethics & Governance: Representation, Accuracy, and Disclosure in AI Media (15 min)
text:
Ethics & Governance: Representation, Accuracy, and Disclosure in AI Media (15 min)
The thread continues. Earlier workshops dealt with AI text and team use. Making images and visuals adds a distinct set of risks, because pictures carry meaning—and bias—in ways that land fast and stick.
Representation bias is the big one. Image models learn from internet-scale pictures and reproduce the stereotypes in them. Ask for “a doctor,” “an epidemiologist,” “a health department director” and you’ll often get the same narrow demographic; ask for “a low-income neighborhood” or “a person struggling with addiction” and you may get stigmatizing, stereotyped scenes. In public health this is not a small problem: biased visuals can misrepresent and stigmatize the very communities we serve, and signal who “belongs” in a profession. Generate a few of these in the activity and you’ll see it directly.
Accuracy and misinformation. A generated image can depict an event that never happened or a “chart” with invented numbers; Acrobat AI Assistant can misread a table or miss a caveat in a PDF. Never present AI-generated imagery as documentary evidence, and verify any figure or claim AI pulls from a document against the source.
Dignity and consent. Avoid generating images that resemble identifiable real people or specific community members, and take extra care depicting vulnerable groups. The communities in your materials should be represented with accuracy and respect—ideally informed by the people themselves, the “nothing about us without us” principle.
Copyright and “commercially safe.” Adobe trains Firefly on licensed and public-domain content and positions output as commercially safe—a real advantage for published reports and campaigns. That is not a blank check: still review each image, don’t prompt to mimic a living artist’s style or real logos and brands, and confirm usage rights for anything consequential.
Disclosure. Label AI-generated visuals according to Drexel and your unit’s norms, especially for public-facing materials. Audiences increasingly expect to know when an image was AI-made.






ImportantQuick Bias Self-Test



Before using a generated image of people, ask: Would the community depicted recognize themselves with dignity, or as a stereotype? Generate the same prompt two or three times—if the demographics never vary, the model is showing you its bias, not reality.

title: Workshop 4: AI for Documents & Visuals (Adobe)
section: Activity 1: Acrobat AI Assistant on a Real PDF
text:
Activity 1: Acrobat AI Assistant on a Real PDF

Activity 1: Acrobat AI Assistant on a Real PDF

Duration: ~19 minutes | Format: Individual, hands-on. Use a non-sensitive PDF or a document appropriate for Adobe’s Drexel-listed Moderate Risk Data classification.

Goal: Turn a long document into something usable in minutes.

Instructions

Open a long PDF in Acrobat — a public report, an article, a grant guideline, or a document appropriate for Adobe’s Moderate Risk Data classification. No high-risk regulated data unless Drexel explicitly approves that use case.
Open AI Assistant and try each:

Summarize: “Give me a one-paragraph summary and then 5 key takeaways.”
Ask: Ask a specific question the document should answer (“What is the eligibility criteria?” / “What does it say about data sharing?”). Notice it cites the passage.
Extract: “List every deadline and requirement mentioned, as a table.”
Repurpose: “Draft a 4-sentence email to my team summarizing what they need to know from this.”

Verify one answer against the source passage it cites. Confirm it didn’t miss or misstate anything.



Reflection

How long would that summary or extraction have taken by hand?
Where did it cite cleanly, and where would you double-check before trusting it?

title: Workshop 4: AI for Documents & Visuals (Adobe)
section: Activity 2: A Communication Graphic in Firefly / Express
text:
Activity 2: A Communication Graphic in Firefly / Express

Activity 2: A Communication Graphic in Firefly / Express

Duration: ~15 minutes | Format: Individual, hands-on

Goal: Produce a clean, shareable graphic for a real communication need.

Instructions

Pick a real need: a flyer for an event, a social post, a simple infographic, or a title visual for a slide or report.
In Adobe Express, start from a template that fits the format (social, flyer, presentation).
Use generative tools where they help:

Text-to-image for a background or illustration (“a diverse group of community members at an outdoor health fair, warm daylight, flat illustration style”).
Generative fill / expand to adjust an existing image to the frame.
Express’s text and layout tools to add your headline and key message.

Keep it readable: one clear message, legible text, accessible color contrast. Generative AI fills space; the communication discipline is still yours.



Reflection

Is this good enough to use, or a starting point for a designer?
Did any generated image misrepresent the community or topic? (A real risk worth naming.)









WarningCheck Before You Publish



Generated images can include inaccurate or stereotyped depictions. For anything public-facing—especially representing communities you serve—review carefully and get a second set of eyes. Credit/disclose AI-generated visuals per Drexel and your unit’s norms.

title: Workshop 2: Workflow Enhancement
text:
Overview (8 min)
Where AI fits. The biggest time savings come from the repetitive middle of a task—the first draft, the summary, the reformat, the “make this clearer.” AI handles that draft; you bring the judgment and the final word.
Copilot vs. ChatGPT Edu—when to use which.

Microsoft 365 Copilot shines when the work is already in a Microsoft file: a Word doc to rewrite, an Outlook thread to summarize, an Excel sheet to analyze. It sees the document you’re in and respects Microsoft 365 permissions.
Copilot Chat with Enterprise Data Protection is useful for web-based drafting, summarizing, and brainstorming when you do not need a reusable GPT.
ChatGPT Edu shines for open-ended drafting, summarizing pasted text, file-based analysis, and building reusable assistants. Use GPT-5.5 when it appears in your model picker; otherwise use the current default/latest GPT model in the Drexel workspace.

Use each tool according to Drexel’s data classifications. See Tools & Access.






TipFind Your Candidates



A task is a good AI candidate if it’s (1) text-heavy, (2) something you do repeatedly, and (3) low-stakes enough that a draft-plus-review is fine. Think: status updates, meeting recaps, first drafts, summaries, reformatting.

title: Workshop 2: Workflow Enhancement
section: Ethics & Governance: Verification, Disclosure, and Over-Reliance (15 min)
text:
Ethics & Governance: Verification, Disclosure, and Over-Reliance (15 min)
The thread continues. Workshop 1 covered how AI fails and who’s accountable. Today: what that accountability means once AI is in your daily work.
Verify before it counts. Speed is the selling point and the trap. AI can fabricate facts, statistics, and citations that read as authoritative. For anything that goes into a real document or decision, check it against the source—especially numbers and references. A quick rule from the intensive course: if you can’t open it, you can’t cite it. Treat every AI-supplied figure as unverified until you’ve confirmed it.
Disclose appropriately. Norms are still forming, but the safe defaults are clear: for official work products, external communications, and anything research-related, be transparent that AI assisted, following Drexel’s AI policy and your unit’s expectations. Disclosure protects your credibility and models the behavior we want across the school. When unsure, ask your supervisor or PI what level of disclosure they expect.
Don’t over-rely. If AI does all the drafting and thinking, two things erode: your own skill and judgment, and your voice. Outputs also drift toward a generic sameness. Use AI to get past the blank page and to pressure-test your thinking—not to replace it. Read what it produces critically, keep your own voice, and stay the expert in the loop.
Confidentiality in everyday workflows. Even approved tools have limits. Microsoft 365 Copilot can see the document and files you have access to—make sure that’s appropriate before you ask it to summarize across them. Match every task to Drexel’s data classification, and never paste regulated data into a tool not cleared for it. See Tools & Access.






ImportantBefore You Hit Send



Three checks on anything AI helped write: Verified? (facts and citations confirmed). Disclosed? (AI use noted where expected). Yours? (it says what you mean, in your voice). If not all three, it’s not ready.

title: Workshop 2: Workflow Enhancement
text:
Activity 1: Microsoft 365 Copilot in Office

Activity 1: Microsoft 365 Copilot in Office

Duration: ~19 minutes | Format: Hands-on, choose by your role

Goal: Use Microsoft 365 Copilot where your work already lives. Pick the track that fits you. Use non-sensitive or appropriately classified content only.

Track A — Word (drafting & rewriting)

Open a Word document with a few paragraphs of real (non-sensitive) text.
Use Copilot to summarize it into key points.
Ask Copilot to rewrite a paragraph for a specific audience (“rewrite for a non-technical reader”).
Ask it to draft a new section from a one-line instruction.



Track B — Outlook (email triage)

Open a long email thread.
Ask Copilot to summarize the thread and list any decisions or action items.
Ask it to draft a reply with a tone you specify (e.g., “concise and friendly, confirm the meeting time”).
Edit and send (or save as draft).



Track C — Excel (quick analysis)

Open a spreadsheet with non-sensitive tabular data (or use sample data).
Ask Copilot to summarize what’s in the data.
Ask for a formula in plain language (“flag rows where the amount is over 5000”).
Ask it to suggest a chart or surface a trend.



Reflection

Did Copilot save time versus doing it by hand? Where did you still need to correct it?
Which of your weekly tasks could this replace the first-draft step for?

title: Workshop 2: Workflow Enhancement
section: Activity 2: ChatGPT Edu for Your Real Task
text:
Activity 2: ChatGPT Edu for Your Real Task

Activity 2: ChatGPT Edu for Your Real Task

Duration: ~13 minutes | Format: Individual, bring your own task

Goal: Run one task you actually need to do this week, start to finish.

Instructions

Pick a real task that is non-sensitive or appropriate for ChatGPT Edu under Drexel’s Terms of Use: a report section, a summary of a long document, a set of meeting notes to clean up, a draft announcement, a literature blurb on a public topic.
In ChatGPT Edu, select GPT-5.5 if it is available; otherwise use the current default/latest GPT model. Write a PTCF prompt (Persona, Task, Context, Format—from Workshop 1). Be specific about the format you want.
Run it. Then iterate: shorten, change tone, add a section, ask for a table. Push it 2–3 rounds until it’s close to usable.
Do the final edit yourself. Note what you changed—that’s the part that needs your expertise.



Two Patterns Worth Knowing
Summarize-then-act. Paste a long document and ask: “Summarize the key points, then draft a 3-sentence email I can send to my team about what they need to do.” One step gets you from raw material to a usable product.
Make-it-a-table. For anything with structure—options, pros and cons, a comparison, a schedule—add: “Put this in a table.” It’s faster to scan and easier to drop into a doc.


Build a Reusable Recipe
Once a prompt works, save it. Strip out the specifics and leave placeholders:

You are a [role]. [Task]. Context: [details]. Format: [structure, length, tone].

Keep these in a running note. Next week you fill in the blanks instead of starting over.








WarningVerify Before It Counts



AI can invent facts, citations, and numbers. For anything that goes into a real document or decision, check it against the source. Never paste sensitive or regulated data into any tool unless that tool and use case are approved for it.

title: AI-Assisted Web & Dashboards
text:
Overview
AI can generate a working dashboard or HTML page from your data and a description — charts, filters, tables — in minutes. You guide the design and verify the numbers.
Anything you publish should use non-sensitive data, and every displayed figure should be checked against the source before sharing.

title: AI-Assisted Web & Dashboards
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Describe a crash dashboard
Time ~20 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu (or Claude) + a browser
Goal. Turn data into a shareable, self-contained dashboard.
Setup. Download the Crash Data 2020–2024 (CSV) — it includes crash coordinates.
Steps.

Describe exactly what you want:

Build a self-contained HTML dashboard from this Philadelphia crash CSV with three views: (1) a map of pedestrian-involved crashes, (2) a bar chart of crashes by year, (3) a table of the top collision types. Use only public libraries from a CDN, and keep all data processing inside the file.

Open the produced HTML file in a browser.
Check one number on the dashboard against the raw data.

Expected result. A working local dashboard (map + chart + table), much like the crash-data explorers already in your course.
Check your work. The “by year” totals should match your earlier counts, and all map points should fall within Philadelphia.
Common pitfalls. Verify every displayed figure. Only use public data in anything you’ll share. Watch for points at (0,0) caused by missing coordinates.
Stretch (optional). Ask AI to add a year filter that updates all three views at once.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Iterate the design
Time ~13 min · Tools same as Activity 1
Goal. Improve a dashboard with small, controlled changes.
Steps.

Make one change at a time:

Change the map to a heatmap of pedestrian crashes.


Add a dropdown to filter by year.


Fix the chart’s axis labels and add a title.

Keep the prompts that worked in a short note for reuse.

Expected result. A cleaner dashboard with a working filter.
Check your work. After each change, confirm the underlying numbers didn’t shift unexpectedly.
Common pitfalls. Changing many things at once makes failures hard to localize — iterate singly.
Stretch (optional). Ask AI to add a short methods note: data source, years covered, and what “pedestrian-involved” means.

title: Statistical Interpretation across Stata, R & Python
text:
Moderate · Data & Analysis track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Data & Analysis track
Read output, translate between languages, and sanity-check what AI tells you.

title: Statistical Interpretation across Stata, R & Python
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Have AI explain model output in plain language
Translate a model or command between Stata, R, and Python
Sanity-check estimates and the assumptions behind them

title: Statistical Interpretation across Stata, R & Python
text:
Overview
AI can explain output, translate a Stata command into R or Python, and suggest diagnostics. It can also be confidently wrong about methods — treat its statistical claims as drafts to verify against a text or a statistician.
Interpretation and assumptions are exactly where your expertise matters most; that part doesn’t transfer to the tool.

title: Statistical Interpretation across Stata, R & Python
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Translate a model across Stata, R, and Python
Time ~20 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu + Stata and/or R/Python
Goal. Move a model between languages and confirm the translation is faithful.
Setup. Download the Crash Data 2020–2024 (CSV). Outcome: a fatal-crash indicator (fatal count > 0). Predictors: a few crash characteristics such as speeding-related, intersection, and a light/dark indicator (use the exact field names from the dataset’s metadata).
Steps.

Start in your home language (Stata, if that’s yours):

Write Stata code for a logistic regression of a fatal-crash indicator on speeding, intersection, and a light/dark indicator using this Philadelphia crash data. Then give the exact equivalent in R (glm) and Python (statsmodels), and interpret the output in plain language.

Run at least two of the three.
Compare:

The R and Stata results should match. Confirm the coefficients, odds ratios, and sample sizes are the same; if they differ, explain why.


Expected result. Matching estimates across languages, plus an odds-ratio interpretation.
Check your work. Ns and coefficients should match across languages (only tiny differences from default options).
Common pitfalls. Different default reference categories or NA handling cause mismatches — check those first. Treat any statistical claim from AI as a draft to verify.
Stretch (optional). Ask which model assumptions matter most here and how to check each.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Interrogate the output
Time ~15 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu + R or Stata
Goal. Decide what the model actually supports — and what it doesn’t.
Setup. Use the model you fit in Activity 1.
Steps.

Paste the output:

Here is my logistic regression output. What assumptions should I check, and how (in R or Stata)? Flag any sign of separation, sparse cells, or multicollinearity.

Run the suggested checks.
Ask for a careful interpretation:

Write a two-sentence, accurate interpretation of the speeding odds ratio for a non-statistical audience — without overclaiming causality.


Expected result. A list of checks you ran, plus an honest, non-causal interpretation.
Check your work. Confirm every claim against the output; reject anything the data don’t support.
Common pitfalls. AI often slips into causal language (“speeding causes…”). This is observational data — keep it associational.
Stretch (optional). Ask AI to name the single biggest threat to validity in this analysis.

title: Responsible AI
text:

title: Responsible AI
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Apply the accountability principle: AI drafts, a human decides
Decide when to disclose AI use
Use a governance framework (WHO, ASPPH) to guide decisions

title: Responsible AI
text:
Overview
Responsible AI isn’t a separate task — it’s how you do every task. The governing principle from Workshop 1 holds: the person who acts on AI output owns the result. Disclosure, verification, and human oversight are the operational habits.
Public-health bodies (WHO, ASPPH) offer frameworks for AI ethics and governance. They translate values — accountability, transparency, equity, safety — into checkable practices.

title: Responsible AI
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Write a disclosure norm
Time ~15 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Decide where and how to disclose AI use.
Setup. List three places AI shows up in your work.
Steps.

For each, decide whether and how you’d disclose AI assistance.
Draft a norm:

Draft a one-paragraph AI-use disclosure norm for our team covering these cases — balanced and practical. [cases]

Refine to fit your context.

Expected result. A short, shareable disclosure norm.
Check your work. Is it specific enough to act on, or just aspirational?
Common pitfalls. Over-disclosing trivial use is noise; under-disclosing consequential use is a problem. Aim for “where it matters.”
Stretch (optional). Add a line on who’s accountable when AI informs a decision.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Apply a governance framework
Time ~15 min · Tools WHO or ASPPH framework + ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Turn principles into practice.
Setup. Skim one framework (e.g., WHO ethics and governance of AI for health).
Steps.

Pick two principles (e.g., transparency, equity).
Make them concrete:

For each principle, give two concrete actions a public-health team could take in everyday AI use. [principles]

Adapt the actions to a real task you do.

Expected result. Two principles turned into actions you’d actually take.
Check your work. Could you tell whether you’re following the action, or is it vague?
Common pitfalls. Frameworks become wallpaper if not operationalized. Make them checkable.
Stretch (optional). Identify one current practice that violates a principle and fix it.

title: Qualitative Data Analysis
text:
Moderate · Data & Analysis track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Data & Analysis track
Use AI to assist coding and themes — without outsourcing your interpretation.

title: Qualitative Data Analysis
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Use AI to propose codes and group themes in open-text data
Build and apply a codebook, then verify agreement
Weigh the debate over AI in qualitative work and protect confidentiality

title: Qualitative Data Analysis
text:
Overview
AI can suggest codes, group themes, and summarize patterns in interview or open-text data quickly. But qualitative scholars warn that outsourcing interpretation strips out the reflexivity that gives the method its meaning. Use AI to assist your reading, not replace it.
Transcripts are often sensitive. De-identify them and use an approved tool — confidentiality comes first.

title: Qualitative Data Analysis
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Draft a codebook from open-text
Time ~18 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu (approved tool for this content)
Goal. Build a codebook from qualitative responses while keeping your interpretation central.
Setup. Use a small, non-sensitive set of open-ended responses. You can use the free-text “description” field from a 311 Service & Information Requests slice, or the eight sample walkability comments below:

“Cars fly down our street, no stop signs.” · “Sidewalks are cracked and hard with a stroller.” · “I feel safe walking to the train in the morning.” · “No crosswalk near the school, kids dart across.” · “Too dark at night, the lights are out.” · “Love the new bump-outs, easier to cross.” · “Trash and parked cars block the corner.” · “The bus stop has nowhere to sit or shelter.”

Steps.

Draft a codebook:

Propose an initial qualitative codebook for these walkability comments: 5–7 codes, each with a name, a one-sentence definition, and an example quote. Note any response that could fit more than one code.

Revise with your own judgment — rename, merge, or split codes. You own the meaning.
Apply it back:

Using my revised codebook, code each comment and show your reasoning for each.


Expected result. A codebook (codes + definitions + examples) that you have revised.
Check your work. Do the codes capture what respondents meant, or just what was easy to label? Adjust accordingly.
Common pitfalls. AI flattens nuance and imposes its own frame — treat it as a first pass, not the analysis. De-identify any real transcripts and use an approved tool.
Stretch (optional). Ask AI to surface a theme across codes, then find a quote that complicates that theme.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Code a new batch and check agreement
Time ~13 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Apply the codebook and check reliability against your own coding.
Setup. A second small batch of ~8 responses (real and de-identified, or another sample set).
Steps.

Have AI code them:

Using this codebook, code each of these new responses and show which code(s) apply and why.

Independently code the same eight yourself.
Compare:

Here is the AI’s coding and mine. Where do we disagree, and what’s the likely reason?


Expected result. A coded batch plus a short list of disagreements.
Check your work. Disagreements should point to fuzzy code definitions — tighten them.
Common pitfalls. High agreement can be false comfort if the codebook is shallow. Your reading is the standard, not the AI’s.
Stretch (optional). Refine the codebook from the disagreements and re-run the batch.

title: Multilingual & Culturally-Adapted Materials
text:
Moderate · Communication & Dissemination track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Communication & Dissemination track
Reach more communities by translating and adapting materials — with a quality check, not blind trust.

title: Multilingual & Culturally-Adapted Materials
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Translate outreach materials into another language with AI
Adapt for cultural context, not just words
Quality-check with back-translation and a human reviewer

title: Multilingual & Culturally-Adapted Materials
text:
Overview
AI translates quickly and can adapt tone for a community, but machine translation makes confident errors — especially with idioms, health terms, and culturally specific concepts. A human speaker should review anything that reaches the public.
Cultural adaptation is more than translation: examples, framing, and imagery may need to change. AI can propose options; community knowledge decides.

title: Multilingual & Culturally-Adapted Materials
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Translate with back-translation QA
Time ~15 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu (or an approved translation tool)
Goal. Translate an outreach message and catch errors before it goes public.
Setup. A short, non-sensitive outreach message (e.g., a crosswalk-safety reminder) and a target language relevant to your community.
Steps.

Translate:

Translate this community health message into [language], keeping a warm, plain-language tone. [message]

In a fresh chat, back-translate:

Translate this [language] text back into English, literally. [translation]

Compare the back-translation to your original and investigate any drift in meaning.

Expected result. A translation plus a back-translation you’ve compared.
Check your work. A bilingual colleague should review anything published — machine translation of health terms is error-prone.
Common pitfalls. Idioms and clinical terms mistranslate confidently. Never publish machine translation of health materials unreviewed.
Stretch (optional). Ask which sentences are most likely to be misunderstood in the target language.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Culturally adapt, not just translate
Time ~13 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Adapt framing and examples for a specific community, not just the words.
Setup. Your translated message plus a brief description of the target community.
Steps.

Surface adaptation needs:

For [community], how might the examples, framing, or imagery in this message land differently? Suggest culturally appropriate adaptations. [message + community]

Draft an adapted version; mark what a community reviewer must confirm.
Check for harm:

Flag anything in my adaptation that could unintentionally stereotype or exclude.


Expected result. An adapted draft plus a list to verify with community members.
Check your work. Cultural knowledge belongs to the community — AI suggestions are hypotheses to confirm, not facts.
Common pitfalls. AI can introduce stereotypes while “adapting.” Review with people from the community.
Stretch (optional). Make two variants and plan a quick community feedback check.

title: MCP & Connecting AI to Data & Tools

Advanced · Technical & Agentic track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Technical & Agentic track
Understand how assistants connect to real systems — and the access risks that come with it.

title: MCP & Connecting AI to Data & Tools
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Explain what MCP (Model Context Protocol) does
Recognize when connecting AI to a tool helps vs. adds risk
Apply least-privilege and approval before connecting to data

title: MCP & Connecting AI to Data & Tools
text:
Overview
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard way to connect an assistant to outside data sources and tools — a file store, a database, a calendar. It’s the plumbing behind agents that do more than chat. Powerful, and a new surface for mistakes and access risk.
Connect with least privilege: give the narrowest access that does the job, prefer read-only, and confirm Drexel’s rules before connecting to any real or sensitive system.

title: MCP & Connecting AI to Data & Tools
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Advanced — Map a connection with least privilege
Time ~15 min · Tools conceptual + the connector’s docs
Goal. Plan a safe AI-to-tool connection.
Setup. Pick a task that would need AI to reach a tool or data source (e.g., read a shared folder of public reports).
Steps.

Write exactly what access the task needs — then the narrowest version that still works (read-only? one folder?).
Get a second opinion:

Here’s the task and the access I’m considering. What’s the least-privilege version, and what could go wrong if it’s over-scoped? [task]

Decide read-only vs. write, and what stays off-limits.

Expected result. A least-privilege access plan.
Check your work. Could a misbehaving connection do damage with this access? If so, narrow it.
Common pitfalls. Convenience tempts over-permissioning. Prefer read-only; confirm Drexel rules before connecting any real or sensitive system.
Stretch (optional). Decide whether the connection is even worth the risk versus a manual copy-paste.

Activity 2 · Advanced — Risk-check the connection
Time ~12 min · Tools conceptual
Goal. Put a human approval step before consequential actions.
Setup. Your plan from Activity 1.
Steps.

List what could go wrong if the connection misbehaves or the AI is misled by content it reads.
Add a human-approval step before any write, send, or delete action.
Find the worst case:

Given this connection, what’s the single highest-risk action, and how do I gate it?


Expected result. A risk list plus an approval gate on the riskiest action.
Check your work. Is there any path where the AI acts irreversibly without a human? Close it.
Common pitfalls. Connected agents can be misled by malicious content (prompt injection) — keep humans on consequential steps.
Stretch (optional). Note which actions you’d never connect to AI at all.

title: Local Models via Ollama
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Run an open model locally with Ollama
Judge when local beats cloud (privacy, cost, offline)
Know the trade-offs in capability and setup

title: Local Models via Ollama
text:
Overview
Tools like Ollama run open-weight models on your own computer — nothing leaves the machine. That’s attractive for sensitive-adjacent work, cost control, or offline settings. The trade-off is capability and setup: local models are smaller and need a capable machine.
Local is not automatically “approved” for sensitive data — confirm with Drexel before using any tool, local or cloud, with protected information.

title: Local Models via Ollama
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Advanced — Run a model locally
Time ~20 min · Tools Ollama + a capable laptop
Goal. Run an open model offline and judge the tradeoff.
Setup. Install Ollama (ollama.com) and pull a small model (e.g., a 7–8B model).
Steps.

Run a basic task entirely offline — summarize a public document:
ollama run <model> then paste the text.
Run the same task on a cloud model (ChatGPT Edu) and compare quality and speed.
Note where the local model is good enough and where it falls short.

Expected result. A working local run plus a quality/speed comparison.
Check your work. Confirm nothing left your machine (that’s the point) — and that Drexel rules still apply for any sensitive data.
Common pitfalls. Local is not automatically approved for sensitive data. Local models are smaller and weaker — set expectations.
Stretch (optional). Write the conditions under which you’d choose local over cloud.

Activity 2 · Advanced — Decide local vs. cloud for a real task
Time ~12 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Make a defensible tool choice.
Setup. A task where privacy, cost, or offline use matters.
Steps.

List the task’s constraints: privacy, cost, connectivity, capability needed.
Argue both sides:

Given these constraints, argue local vs. cloud for this task. What would change the decision?

Decide, and note the data-rule check.

Expected result. A reasoned local-vs-cloud decision.
Check your work. Did you confirm the data classification regardless of where the model runs?
Common pitfalls. “It’s local, so it’s fine” is not a data-rule exemption.
Stretch (optional). Estimate the capability gap for your specific task by testing both.

title: Keeping Your Judgment: Cognitive Offloading & When to Scrutinize AI
text:
Moderate · Governance, Ethics & Equity track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Governance, Ethics & Equity track
Use AI to amplify your judgment, not replace it — and know where it needs closer scrutiny.

title: Keeping Your Judgment: Cognitive Offloading & When to Scrutinize AI
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Explain cognitive offloading and the risks of skill atrophy and automation bias
Decide where AI needs close scrutiny versus where it’s low-stakes
Build habits that use AI for productivity while keeping your judgment, taste, and voice sharp

title: Keeping Your Judgment: Cognitive Offloading & When to Scrutinize AI
text:
Overview
These tools are for productivity — getting past the blank page, drafting, summarizing, pressure-testing your thinking, accelerating the repetitive middle of a task. They are not a substitute for your expertise. The skill that matters most in the AI era is judgment: knowing what’s right, what’s worth questioning, and what only a human should decide. The rule from across this series holds — AI drafts; you verify and decide. Use it as a first draft and a thought partner, never the final word.
Cognitive offloading is handing your thinking to a tool. Some is healthy — we offload arithmetic to calculators. Too much erodes the very skill and judgment you need to catch the tool’s mistakes, and your voice drifts toward a generic sameness. The goal is calibrated reliance: lean on AI for low-stakes, reversible, checkable work, and keep your hands on the high-stakes, novel, and nuanced. Taste — knowing when an output is good, off, or subtly wrong — is something you keep by staying in the work, not by outsourcing all of it.






TipA quick test for any task



Before you hand something to AI, ask: Reversible? (could I undo a wrong result) · Verifiable? (can I check it) · Low-stakes? (no one’s care, rights, or resources ride on it). The more “no” answers, the more scrutiny it needs — or the more it should stay human.

title: Keeping Your Judgment: Cognitive Offloading & When to Scrutinize AI
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Map where AI needs scrutiny
Time ~18 min · Tools a doc or paper + ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Build a personal map of where to lean on AI and where to scrutinize it closely.
Setup. List 8–10 tasks you do regularly.
Steps.

Rate each task 1–5 on three things: stakes (who’s harmed if it’s wrong), reversibility (can you undo it), and verifiability (can you check it easily).
Sort them into two columns — lean on AI (low-stakes, reversible, checkable) vs. keep human / scrutinize closely (high-stakes, irreversible, hard to verify).
Pressure-test your map:

Here are my tasks and how I rated their stakes, reversibility, and verifiability. Which did I likely mis-rate, and which deserve closer human scrutiny than I gave them?

Assign each task a scrutiny level: skim, spot-check, verify everything, or don’t automate.

Expected result. A personal task map with a scrutiny level for each.
Check your work. Would you defend your “lean on AI” calls if one went wrong in public?
Common pitfalls. The riskiest tasks often feel routine. High stakes + irreversible + hard to verify means scrutinize closely or keep it human.
Stretch (optional). Pick one “keep human” task and write why it needs your judgment specifically — that sentence is your taste, made explicit.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Do it yourself first, then compare
Time ~15 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Use AI in a way that sharpens your skill instead of eroding it.
Setup. A task you’d normally hand straight to AI — a short analysis, a paragraph, a plan.
Steps.

Do a rough version yourself first (about 5 minutes, no AI).
Then ask AI for its version of the same task.
Compare honestly:

Here’s my draft and your draft. What did you do better, what did I do better, and what did I catch that you missed?

Build the final from the best of both, and note what you learned.

Expected result. A stronger final product plus a note on what you’d have missed by offloading entirely.
Check your work. Did doing it yourself first surface something the AI got wrong, generic, or bland?
Common pitfalls. Always starting from AI’s draft anchors you to its framing and quietly erodes your skill and voice. Do the thinking first, at least sometimes.
Stretch (optional). Name one skill you don’t want to lose and protect it — keep doing that one unaided on a regular basis.

title: Grant & Proposal Development
text:
Moderate · Communication & Dissemination track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Communication & Dissemination track
Draft and tighten the recurring pieces of a proposal — with your science and your verification.

title: Grant & Proposal Development
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Draft specific aims, summaries, and budget justifications with AI
Tighten language to a funder’s guidelines and page limits
Verify every claim, citation, and number before it goes in

title: Grant & Proposal Development
text:
Overview
AI can draft a specific-aims page, a summary, or a budget justification from your notes, and reshape text to a funder’s format. The science, the feasibility, and the citations are yours to get right.
Funders increasingly expect disclosure of AI assistance and prohibit fabricated content. Treat AI output as a first draft you verify line by line.

title: Grant & Proposal Development
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Draft a Specific Aims page
Time ~20 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Go from rough notes to a structured Aims draft you then make your own.
Setup. Your project’s goal, gap, and approach (non-sensitive). Example topic: reducing pedestrian injuries near Philadelphia schools through built-environment changes.
Steps.

Ask for a structured draft:

You are an experienced NIH grant writer. From these notes, draft a one-page Specific Aims: an opening hook, the gap, long-term goal, central hypothesis, and 2–3 aims with brief sub-aims. Notes: [goal, gap, approach].

Rewrite the science in your own words — the hypothesis and aims must be yours, not the model’s.
Get a reviewer’s-eye critique:

Critique my revised Aims as a study-section reviewer would: what’s underdeveloped, what’s overreaching, what’s missing?


Expected result. A one-page Aims draft plus a reviewer-style critique.
Check your work. Verify every citation and statistic you add against the actual source.
Common pitfalls. AI invents plausible citations and overpromises feasibility. You own the science and the claims; disclose AI assistance per funder policy.
Stretch (optional). Ask for a 3-sentence project summary derived from the Aims.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Fit a section to the funder’s rules
Time ~15 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Tighten text to guidelines and page limits without dropping required content.
Setup. A draft section (e.g., a significance paragraph or budget justification) plus the funder’s instructions.
Steps.

Ask it to check compliance and tighten:

Here is my draft and the funder’s formatting and length rules. Flag where I don’t meet the guidance and tighten the text to fit — without dropping anything required. [draft] [rules]

Confirm nothing required was cut.
Surface gaps:

List anything the guidelines require that my draft is still missing.


Expected result. A tightened, compliant section plus a gap checklist.
Check your work. Re-read against the funder’s actual instructions yourself — AI can miss a requirement.
Common pitfalls. Trimming can quietly remove mandatory elements; verify.
Stretch (optional). Draft a cover-letter paragraph on the proposal’s fit to the funding opportunity.

title: Geospatial / GIS & Built Environment
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Use AI to scaffold mapping and spatial-join code
Work with street-level and satellite imagery responsibly
Validate spatial results and watch for ecological fallacy

title: Geospatial / GIS & Built Environment
text:
Overview
AI can scaffold geospatial code (mapping, buffers, spatial joins) and help interpret street-level or satellite imagery — directly useful for built-environment and environmental-health work. As with all coding, you read and validate what it produces.
Spatial work has its own traps: projection errors, edge effects, and the ecological fallacy. AI won’t catch these for you; methodological judgment does.

title: Geospatial / GIS & Built Environment
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Advanced — Scaffold and validate a spatial join
Time ~22 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu/Claude + R (sf) or Python (geopandas)
Goal. Build and validate a spatial analysis with AI help.
Setup. Two public spatial layers — e.g., Philadelphia crash points (the Philadelphia Crashes data GeoJSON) and a neighborhood or Census-tract boundary file.
Steps.

Scaffold the code:

Write R (sf) or Python (geopandas) code to load these two layers, ensure a matching CRS, and count pedestrian crashes per neighborhood. Explain each step and the CRS handling. [datasets]

Run it and validate against a known case — does a high-crash corridor show up where you’d expect?
Check spatial traps:

What projection or edge-effect issues should I check here?


Expected result. A crashes-per-area count and a validation against a known pattern.
Check your work. CRS mismatches silently produce wrong joins — confirm both layers share a projection.
Common pitfalls. Don’t infer individual risk from area rates (the ecological fallacy). AI won’t catch spatial traps for you.
Stretch (optional). Map the result and sanity-check a few points against the source.

Activity 2 · Advanced — Interpret street imagery carefully
Time ~15 min · Tools a multimodal AI + public Street View
Goal. Use AI on street imagery while respecting its limits.
Setup. A few public Google Street View images of Philadelphia intersections (faces and plates are already blurred).
Steps.

Ask for a description:

Describe the pedestrian-safety features visible in this street image: crosswalks, signals, curb ramps, obstructions.

Note where it guessed or hedged.
Mark what a human rater must confirm before the labels are usable.

Expected result. AI-described features and a list needing human confirmation.
Check your work. Vision models guess confidently — verify each safety-relevant call.
Common pitfalls. Don’t treat AI image descriptions as measurements. Avoid images with identifiable people in unapproved tools.
Stretch (optional). Compare the AI’s description to a quick manual audit of the same image.

title: AI for Design & Visual Communication
section: What you’ll be able to do

What you’ll be able to do

Generate communication graphics (infographics, social posts, slides) with AI design tools
Choose the right tool — Claude, Canva, Figma, or Adobe Express/Firefly — for the job and the data level
Apply accuracy, accessibility, representation, and disclosure standards to AI-made visuals

title: AI for Design & Visual Communication
text:
Overview
Several AI tools now produce usable visuals fast. Claude can generate interactive infographics and web pages (“artifacts”); Canva and Figma have AI design features; Adobe Express with Firefly makes commercially-safe images and templates. You bring the message, the accuracy, and the design judgment. This is the self-paced companion to Workshop 4, which focuses on the Adobe tools.
The data rules differ by tool. Adobe Express, Firefly, and Acrobat are Drexel-approved for Moderate Risk data; Canva, Figma, and Claude are consumer tools — Low Risk Data only unless Drexel approves them. Use public, non-sensitive content, disclose AI-generated visuals, and check representation and accessibility before anything is published.

title: AI for Design & Visual Communication
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Build a one-page infographic
Time ~20 min · Tools Claude (or Canva)
Goal. Turn a finding into a clean, accessible visual you can share.
Setup. One public finding — e.g., “In Philadelphia, pedestrian-involved crashes peak between 4–7 PM and are more common on wide arterial roads.” Use public, non-sensitive content only.
Steps.

Describe the infographic (in Claude, ask for an artifact):

Create a clean, accessible one-page infographic (HTML) for this public-health finding: [finding]. Include a headline, one simple chart, and three takeaways. Use high-contrast colors and large text. Public data only.

(In Canva, use Magic Design / Magic Media with a similar prompt.)
Review for accuracy and accessibility — correct chart type, no fake precision, strong contrast, a caption or alt text.
Iterate one change at a time (simplify the chart, fix a label, adjust contrast).

Expected result. A shareable infographic draft.
Check your work. Is every number accurate, the text high-contrast, and a caption/alt text provided?
Common pitfalls. AI visuals can misrepresent data (wrong chart, invented precision) — verify against the source. Consumer design tools are Low Risk data only; use public content.
Stretch (optional). Export it and confirm it meets accessibility basics (color contrast, alt text) for publication.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Match the tool to the job and the data
Time ~15 min · Tools Claude + Canva or Figma + Adobe Express
Goal. Pick the right design tool for the task and the data sensitivity.
Setup. A real visual need — a flyer, a social post, or a slide.
Steps.

Make a quick draft in two tools (e.g., a flyer in Adobe Express, an interactive chart in Claude).
Compare speed, control, output quality, and the data classification each tool allows.
Get a recommendation:

This is [data sensitivity] content for [use]. Which tool fits best and why? List the Drexel data-rule consideration for each of Adobe Express, Canva, Figma, and Claude.


Expected result. A reasoned tool choice with the data-rule rationale.
Check your work. Did you match the tool to Drexel’s classification — Adobe Express/Firefly (Moderate Risk approved) vs. Canva/Figma/Claude (Low Risk only)?
Common pitfalls. The flashiest tool isn’t always approved for your data. Always disclose AI-generated visuals.
Stretch (optional). Decide which tool you’d standardize on for routine team graphics, and why.

title: Data Security & Classification Deep-Dive
text:
Novice · Governance, Ethics & Equity track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Governance, Ethics & Equity track
The rule under every other topic: the right tool for the data’s sensitivity.

title: Data Security & Classification Deep-Dive
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Classify data by sensitivity (public → high-risk)
Match each tool to Drexel’s approved classification
De-identify or substitute when a tool isn’t cleared

title: Data Security & Classification Deep-Dive
text:
Overview
Every AI decision starts with one question: how sensitive is this data, and is this tool approved for it? Drexel publishes which tools may be used with which data levels. Identifiable records, PHI, protected student data, and contract material have strict limits.
When a tool isn’t cleared for your data, the move is to de-identify, use synthetic data, or switch to an approved tool — not to make an exception.

title: Data Security & Classification Deep-Dive
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Novice — Classify and match
Time ~15 min · Tools Drexel AI Tools page + ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Map your data to the right tools.
Setup. List three data types you actually work with.
Steps.

For each, note its sensitivity (public → high-risk) and which Drexel-approved tools allow it (check the AI Tools page).
Sanity-check with AI:

I have these data types and these tools available. Which uses are appropriate and which aren’t, based on data sensitivity? [types] [tools]

Flag any current habit that doesn’t match.

Expected result. A data-to-tool matrix and a list of habits to change.
Check your work. Verify against Drexel’s official AI Tools page, not the AI’s guess.
Common pitfalls. “It’s probably fine” isn’t a classification. When unsure, treat data as more sensitive.
Stretch (optional). Write your team’s one-page “what goes where” cheat sheet.

Activity 2 · Novice — Substitute safely
Time ~12 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu (fabricated data only)
Goal. Make sensitive data safe for a given tool.
Setup. A fabricated sensitive example.
Steps.

De-identify it:

Here is a record with identifiers. Produce a de-identified version safe for a Low-Risk tool, and list what you removed or generalized. [fabricated record]

Check: could the remaining fields still identify someone in combination?
Decide whether de-identification is enough or you need an approved tool.

Expected result. A de-identified version plus a re-identification check.
Check your work. Quasi-identifiers (age + ZIP + date) can re-identify — scrutinize them.
Common pitfalls. Removing names isn’t de-identification; combinations matter.
Stretch (optional). Try generating fully synthetic data instead (see the Methods track).

title: Data Analysis Advanced
text:
Advanced · Data & Analysis track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Data & Analysis track
Scaffold full pipelines with AI — then audit the subtle failure modes.

title: Data Analysis Advanced
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Use AI to scaffold a multistep modeling pipeline
Audit AI-built pipelines for leakage, validation, and metric choice
Know the limits of AI on tabular and time-series data

title: Data Analysis Advanced
text:
Overview
For advanced work, AI can scaffold a full pipeline — feature prep, modeling, evaluation. The failure modes get subtler: data leakage, the wrong validation strategy, overconfident metrics. Your job is to audit each step and the assumptions behind it.
Recent work cautions that “foundation models” don’t automatically win on tabular or time-series data — classic methods often match or beat them. Skepticism is part of the method.

title: Data Analysis Advanced
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Advanced — Audit a modeling pipeline
Time ~25 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu (or Claude) + R or Python
Goal. Build a pipeline, then rigorously audit it for the errors that scale.
Setup. Download the Crash Data 2020–2024 (CSV). Outcome: a serious-crash indicator (fatal or suspected-serious injury). Use only fields known at the time of the crash.
Steps.

Scaffold it:

Build a complete R or Python pipeline that predicts a serious-crash indicator (fatal or major injury) from crash characteristics in this Philadelphia data: load, clean, split, fit a logistic model, and report performance. Use only fields known before or at the time of the crash.

Audit it, point by point:

Leakage — are any predictors really the outcome in disguise (injury counts, fatality flags)? Remove them.
Validation — is the split appropriate? Prefer a temporal split (train on earlier years, test on later).
Metric — serious crashes are rare, so accuracy misleads. Look at recall, precision, and PR-AUC.


Audit this pipeline for data leakage, an inappropriate validation split, and a misleading metric given class imbalance. Show me exactly where each problem is and the minimal fix.

Fix one real flaw and re-run.

Expected result. A pipeline with at least one substantive flaw found and corrected, and honest metrics.
Check your work. Confirm no predictor encodes the outcome, and that the test set is genuinely held out (ideally later years).
Common pitfalls. Leakage inflates performance and hides easily. High accuracy on a rare outcome is meaningless. This is observational data — don’t imply causation.
Stretch (optional). Add calibration (are the predicted probabilities trustworthy?) and report it.

Activity 2 · Advanced — Compare a baseline to a complex model
Time ~20 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu (or Claude) + R or Python
Goal. Decide whether model complexity actually earns its place.
Setup. Same outcome and temporal split as Activity 1.
Steps.

Compare:

Compare a simple logistic-regression baseline to a gradient-boosted model on the same temporal split, reporting PR-AUC and calibration. Tell me whether the complex model is justified.

Inspect the comparison yourself — don’t accept the verdict blindly.
Write one paragraph justifying your choice, weighing interpretability, performance, and maintenance.

Expected result. A side-by-side comparison and your reasoned decision.
Check your work. Is the performance gap real and meaningful, or within noise? Would you trust the complex model in practice?
Common pitfalls. A higher AUC doesn’t automatically justify complexity. Recent evidence shows classic methods often match boosting and “foundation” models on tabular data.
Stretch (optional). Log your prompts, package versions, and a random seed so a colleague could reproduce the comparison (see the Reproducible AI topic).

title: Creating & Using Loops
text:
Advanced · Technical & Agentic track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Technical & Agentic track
Run the same step over a batch — the power and the risk of compounding errors.

title: Creating & Using Loops
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Set up an iterative loop over many items
Add checks so a small early error doesn’t compound
Decide when a loop should be supervised vs. fully manual

title: Creating & Using Loops
text:
Overview
A loop applies the same step across many items — summarize each of 50 documents, code each of 200 responses. It’s where AI saves the most time and where errors scale fastest: one flawed step repeats everywhere.
The discipline is sampling and spot-checking: validate the loop on a few items, run it, then audit a random sample of the output.

title: Creating & Using Loops
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Advanced — Loop with a checkpoint
Time ~20 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu/Claude (+ R/Python if scripting)
Goal. Apply one task across many items, then audit the results.
Setup. About 10 non-sensitive items and one task. You can use 10 short 311 Service & Information Requests descriptions and classify each by issue type.
Steps.

Define the task for a single item and confirm it’s right.
Run the loop over all 10 (in the tool, or with a script that calls the model).
Audit a sample:

Audit a random sample of 3 of these outputs for errors and tell me what’s likely systematically wrong.

Fix the instruction and re-run only the failures.

Expected result. 10 processed items plus an audit of a random sample.
Check your work. A small early error repeats across the whole batch — sampling catches it.
Common pitfalls. Don’t trust a loop just because it ran. Higher stakes mean reviewing every item, not a sample.
Stretch (optional). Add a confidence flag so low-certainty items get human review.

Activity 2 · Advanced — Decide the guardrail for scale
Time ~12 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu/Claude
Goal. Set the rule for when a loop needs full human review.
Setup. A real task you’d loop.
Steps.

Write the rule: review every item vs. a sample, tied to stakes and reversibility.
Pressure-test it:

Give me a scenario where sampling would be dangerous for this task.

Decide and document.

Expected result. A documented review rule for looped tasks.
Check your work. Would you defend this rule if one bad output reached a real decision?
Common pitfalls. Sampling is fine for low-stakes work; never for irreversible or rights-affecting outputs.
Stretch (optional). Add logging so you can reconstruct what the loop did (see Reproducible AI).

title: Coding with AI
text:

title: Coding with AI
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Generate, explain, debug, and refactor code with AI
Review AI-written code critically before running it
Know what must never go into a code prompt

title: Coding with AI
text:
Overview
AI is a capable pair-programmer: it drafts, explains, and fixes code fast. The risk is running code you don’t understand. Read it, test it on a small case, and keep the parts that matter under your eye.
Use approved tools, and never paste sensitive data, credentials, or API keys into a prompt.

title: Coding with AI
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Explain and fix a broken script
Time ~18 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu + R (or Python)
Goal. Use AI to debug code you actually understand before trusting it.
Setup. Download the Crash Data 2020–2024 (CSV). Below is a short R script with a deliberate bug — it should count pedestrian-involved crashes but returns 0 (wrong column name, and NAs not handled):
library(tidyverse)
crashes <- read_csv("crashes.csv")
ped <- crashes %>% filter(PEDESTRIAN > 0)   # column may be named differently; NAs ignored
nrow(ped)
Steps.

Paste the script, the column names, and the wrong result into ChatGPT Edu:

This R script should count Philadelphia crashes involving a pedestrian, but it returns 0. Here are the script and the actual column names. Explain what’s wrong and show the minimal change — don’t rewrite the whole thing.

Apply the fix (likely the correct field name + NA handling) and run it.
Confirm the fix is actually correct:

How do I confirm this fix is right, not just non-erroring?


Expected result. A correct pedestrian-crash count that matches the number from the Data Analysis Basics page.
Check your work. Compare against your earlier count. “No error” is not the same as “correct.”
Common pitfalls. Never paste credentials or non-public data into a prompt. Always verify the output number, not just that the code ran.
Stretch (optional). Ask AI to add a guard that warns if the pedestrian field contains unexpected values.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Refactor with a sanity check
Time ~15 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu + R or Python
Goal. Improve working code and add a test that would actually catch a mistake.
Steps.

Refactor:

Refactor this into a function crashes_by_year(df, count_field) that returns a tidy yearly count, with input checks. Also write a small sanity-check test using a tiny fake data frame with known values.

Run the test and confirm it passes.
Note exactly what changed and why you accepted it.

Expected result. A reusable function plus a passing test.
Check your work. Run the function on the real crash data — totals should match prior results. Then break the function on purpose; the test should fail. If it still passes, the test is too weak.
Common pitfalls. A test that can’t fail is useless. Read the refactored code; don’t run what you don’t understand.
Stretch (optional). Ask for the same function in the other language (R↔︎Python) and confirm both return identical counts.

title: AI Bias & Health Equity Audit
text:
Moderate · Governance, Ethics & Equity track · ~1 hour, self-paced
← Back to the Governance, Ethics & Equity track
Bias in AI becomes inequity in health. Learn to test for it, not assume it away.

title: AI Bias & Health Equity Audit
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Explain where bias enters AI systems
Test outputs across groups, not just overall
Decide when a biased tool shouldn’t be used

title: AI Bias & Health Equity Audit
text:
Overview
AI learns from data that carries society’s inequities, so outputs can reflect and amplify them — and in health, that becomes unequal care. A landmark case: an algorithm used healthcare cost as a proxy for need, rating Black patients as healthier than equally sick White patients (Obermeyer et al., 2019).
The countermeasure is auditing: don’t judge a tool on average performance; test it across the groups you serve, and be willing to not use it.

title: AI Bias & Health Equity Audit
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Audit outputs across groups
Time ~18 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu or an image tool
Goal. Test for disparities, not just average quality.
Setup. An AI output that varies by input — generated example “patients,” summaries, or images. Use non-sensitive, fabricated inputs.
Steps.

Vary the group in the input, holding everything else constant:

Generate a short patient vignette for a [group] person with [condition]. (repeat across groups)

Compare outputs for stereotypes, tone, or quality gaps.
Document any disparity you find.

Expected result. A small audit showing whether outputs differ by group.
Check your work. Are the differences justified by the input, or are they stereotypes?
Common pitfalls. Averages hide disparities — test across the groups you serve.
Stretch (optional). Connect to a real case (e.g., Obermeyer 2019, cost-as-proxy) and name the proxy risk in your own work.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Set a “don’t use” threshold
Time ~12 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Decide when a biased tool shouldn’t be used.
Setup. A real use case.
Steps.

Write what level of disparity would make you not use the tool.
Identify who decides:

Here’s my use case and threshold. Who should be at the table for this decision, and what evidence would I need? [case]

Note the stakeholders and the evidence required.

Expected result. A decision rule plus the people who should make it.
Check your work. Would you defend the threshold to the affected community?
Common pitfalls. “It’s mostly accurate” can still be inequitable. Equity is a go/no-go, not a footnote.
Stretch (optional). Draft a short bias-audit checklist your team can reuse.

title: AI Workshops for DSPH
text:
Welcome
This is a growing series of short, hands-on workshops introducing AI tools for staff and faculty at the Dornsife School of Public Health. Each workshop runs one hour: a brief overview followed by hands-on activities you can complete in the session. Three workshops run this summer, with more scheduled for fall—an Adobe documents-and-visuals session, an intro to agentic AI, and a session on AI for curriculum development.
The series is designed for everyone at DSPH—administrative staff, research staff, and faculty. No prior experience with AI is required. The focus is practical: what these tools can do, how to use them to save time on real work, and how to bring them into team projects.
Prefer to learn at your own pace? The new Self-Paced Tracks turn these topics into self-guided pages with practice activities, readiness quizzes, and curated readings—starting with a full Data & Analysis track.






ImportantUse AI to amplify your judgment, not replace it



The throughline across every workshop and track is the same: AI is a productivity tool, not a substitute for your expertise. Use it to get past the blank page, draft, summarize, and pressure-test your thinking—then bring your own judgment, taste, and accountability to the result. The goal is to develop the judgment for where AI needs close scrutiny (high-stakes, irreversible, or hard-to-verify work) and where it doesn’t—and to watch for cognitive offloading, handing over so much thinking that your own skills and voice quietly erode. More in Keeping Your Judgment: Cognitive Offloading & When to Scrutinize AI.








NoteSeries Details




Format: Three one-hour hands-on workshops (one hour each), bring a laptop
Audience: DSPH staff (administrative and research) and faculty
Dates: June 29, July 7, and July 14, 2026 — all 1:00–2:00 PM
Location: TBD
Recording: Sessions will be recorded and shared after the workshops
Repeats: Additional sessions in summer or fall; series repeats in fall
Facilitator: D. Alex Quistberg, PhD, MPH (daq26@drexel.edu)

title: Building AI Agents with Natural Language
section: What you’ll be able to do
text:
What you’ll be able to do

Assemble an agent from instructions, knowledge, and tools
Set guardrails and human-approval points
Decide what should — and shouldn’t — be handed to an agent

title: Building AI Agents with Natural Language
text:
Overview
An agent takes a goal and carries out several steps toward it, checking in as needed. In ChatGPT Edu you can build one from instructions plus a knowledge file — no code. The building blocks are the same everywhere: instructions, knowledge, tools, skills.
Autonomy raises the stakes. Keep agents on low-stakes, reversible, checkable work, and keep a human approval step before anything consequential.

title: Building AI Agents with Natural Language
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Build a simple assistant
Time ~18 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Assemble a working assistant from instructions plus knowledge.
Setup. A repeated task and one non-sensitive reference file (a template or style guide).
Steps.

Create a custom GPT/assistant with clear instructions — role, behavior, output format, limits — and upload the reference file.
Give it a small multi-step task and watch how it plans.
Review each step, not just the final output.

Expected result. An assistant that completes a multi-step task with your review at each step.
Check your work. Did it follow the limits you set? Try an input that should make it stop and ask.
Common pitfalls. Errors compound across steps. Keep it on low-stakes, reversible work. You own the outcome.
Stretch (optional). Add a knowledge file and see how it changes the assistant’s answers.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Set and test the guardrails
Time ~12 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Define limits and confirm they hold.
Setup. Your assistant from Activity 1.
Steps.

Write its guardrails: what it must never do, and when to stop and ask a human.
Find blind spots:

Given these guardrails, what edge cases might I be missing?

Test a case that should trigger a stop — confirm it does.

Expected result. A guardrail list and a passed stop-test.
Check your work. If it proceeds when it should stop, tighten the instructions.
Common pitfalls. Vague guardrails fail silently. Be explicit about high-stakes triggers.
Stretch (optional). Write the one rule that, if it applied, would mean the task should never be automated.

title: Collaborating on Projects
text:
Overview
A shared Project in ChatGPT Edu gives a team one place for related chats, files, and instructions, so everyone works from the same context and a colleague can pick up where you left off. Custom GPTs turn a good prompt into a reusable team tool, and a shared prompt library spreads what works. This is the self-paced companion to Workshop 3 — Collaboration on Projects; do that workshop for the live, hands-on version.
Shared use raises new questions: whose data is this, who agreed to it, and who has access. Keep sharing inside Drexel (“Only those invited”), keep PHI and sensitive or identifiable data out of shared files, and match the tool to Drexel’s data classification.

title: Collaborating on Projects
text:
Practice activities
Activity 1 · Moderate — Build and share a Project
Time ~18 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Create a shared team workspace a colleague can step into.
Setup. A real team effort (a brief, a report, an onboarding guide). Use non-sensitive or approved files only.
Steps.

Create a Project and add project instructions — the standing context everyone should get automatically:

This project supports the DSPH [team] working on [goal]. Always write in plain, non-technical language. For summaries, use bullets and a one-line bottom line. Flag anything that needs a human decision. Never include identifying or sensitive data.

Upload one or two approved reference files (a template, a style guide, a public report). Start a chat inside the project: “Using the uploaded template, draft a project update for this month.”
Open share settings and invite a colleague with “Only those invited.” Choose Can chat or Can edit, and have them continue your chat.

Expected result. A shared Project your partner can pick up with the same context.
Check your work. Does the partner get your files and instructions without re-pasting anything?
Common pitfalls. Use “Only those invited,” never “Anyone with a link,” for Drexel work. No PHI or sensitive data in shared files. Keep sharing inside Drexel.
Stretch (optional). Add a second reference file and watch how the project’s answers change.

Activity 2 · Moderate — Build a reusable custom GPT
Time ~15 min · Tools ChatGPT Edu
Goal. Turn a repeated team task into a shared tool.
Setup. A task your team does over and over — a standard recap, a plain-language rewriter, a grant-deadline checker.
Steps.

Create a custom GPT with clear instructions:

You are a [role] for the DSPH [team]. When given [input], always produce [output] in this format: [structure]. Use a [tone] tone. If key information is missing, ask for it first. Never include sensitive or identifying data.

Optionally add an approved reference file (a style guide or template). Test with two different inputs; refine where it drifts.
Note how to share it inside your Drexel workspace — and don’t share outside Drexel.

Expected result. A working custom GPT your team can reuse.
Check your work. Does it hold the format and tone across two very different inputs?
Common pitfalls. Don’t add PHI, sensitive PII, or confidential material to GPT files. Share inside Drexel only.
Stretch (optional). Start a shared prompt library with 2–3 entries (title, prompt with placeholders, “use when”).
